meta.note: DIFFERENT from other genres. Narrative is textural, not architectural: a story lives on the student's own voice. So each stage gives a LIGHT FRAME (skeletal opener with a gap, rotates on chevron) PLUS INGREDIENT BANKS (raw material the student assembles themselves). The 'recipe' per stage names the ingredient categories on the card (e.g. adjective + sound + place) — that IS the teaching pattern. Four FIXED stages in sequence (not swappable): Scene, Develop, Tension, Resolution. Connectives are TIME markers, not point-signposts. VOCABULARY IS DRAFT — Majid to refine to his own selection.
meta.usage: The deck does NOT hand over finished sentences. Frame scaffolds the student who is stuck; ingredients personalise so the sentence is theirs.

scene.label: Set the Scene
scene.purpose: Open the story. Establish when, where, and the mood — pull the reader in.
frames: Nobody could have guessed how that ... ... would end.

Time / place: in a town I had never seen before
Sensory details: the smell of rain on warm stone
the taste of salt in the air
not a sound but my own breathing

develop.label: Develop the Action
develop.purpose: Move the story forward. Link events in sequence and let actions reveal feeling.
frames: By the time ... , it was clear that ...

Show-don't-tell details: my hands would not keep still
I kept checking over my shoulder
the silence pressed in on me
I forced a smile I did not feel
every step felt heavier than the last

tension.label: Build the Tension
tension.purpose: The turning point. Break the rhythm, sharpen the verbs, raise the stakes.

resolution.label: Resolve & Reflect
resolution.purpose: Bring the story to rest. Resolve the action, then reflect on what it meant.
frames: It was only later that I understood ...

Reflections: nothing would ever feel quite the same
I had learned something I could not put into words
it had changed how I saw everything
I knew I had been lucky
